entries.body: The City of Albuquerque Division of Child and Family Development (DCFD) Main Office is located at 1820 Randolph Rd S.E., Albuquerque, New Mexico 87106. Main phone: 505-767-6500. Fax: 767-6525. Division Manager: Shana Runck (767-6560). Office hours: Monday – Friday 8:00 a.m. – 4:30 p.m.

Preschool, NM Pre-K and Early Pre-K: Debra Montano, Program Manager (767-6534); Eileen Lucero, Education Specialist (767-6533); Lisa Clover, Education Specialist (767-6518); Patty Besse, Education Specialist (767-6517). Preschool and NM Pre-K Enrollment: Lisa Lopez, Enrollment Specialist (767-6504); Phyllis Santillanes, Enrollment Admin Assistant (767-6566). Main Office/Fax: 767-6500 / 767-6525.

entries.title: Program History and Overview
entries.body: The City of Albuquerque (CABQ) established the Division of Child and Family Development Program (DCFD) in 1989. The first four child development centers were established in collaboration with the Albuquerque Public Schools. The program has expanded to twenty six (26) child development centers, located in elementary schools and other community settings. Preschool (established 1989) serves 356 children in 21 centers, ages 3-5. Early Head Start (established 1994, first grant 1995) serves 128 children ages 0-3 in 7 sites and home visiting programs. NM Pre-K (established 2005) serves 272 children ages 4 in 15 centers. NM Early Pre-K (established 2015) serves 44 children age 3 in 3 centers.

entries.title: NAEYC Accreditation and 5-Star Status
entries.body: CABQ centers have received Accreditation by the National Association for the Education of Young Children (NAEYC). Centers go through accreditation after one year of operation. The State of New Mexico recognizes accredited centers as "5-Star Centers" on its star status scale of 1-5. The state's FOCUS: Essential Elements of Quality (TQRIS) framework, described at www.earlylearningnm.org, determines a program's STAR Level — FOCUS Levels 3, 4, and 5 correspond to 3, 4, or 5 STARs.

entries.title: Hours of Operation by Program
entries.body: Hours vary by center. Early Head Start (ages 0-3): Child Development Center hours are either 8:00 am – 4:30 pm or 7:00 am – 5:30 pm; EHS program hours 8:00 am – 2:30 pm; CABQ Extended Care available before and after the program day. Preschool (ages 3-5) and NM Pre-K (ages 3 or 4): Center hours either 7:00 am – 5:30 pm or 8:00 am – 4:30 pm; Preschool program hours 7:00 am – 5:30 pm or 8:00am – 4:30pm; NM Pre-K program hours 8:00 am – 2:30 pm; CABQ Extended Care for NM Pre-K available before and after the program day.

entries.title: Closure Dates
entries.body: A list of closure dates for city holidays, staff development and center maintenance is available from the enrollment office during the enrollment process and from the Head Teacher or Teacher at the center. These dates may vary from one school year to the next; please request a copy of these dates so that you know in advance when the centers will be closed. Please provide this information to people on your pick-up list.

entries.title: Snow Days and Weather Closures
entries.body: When it snows, parents should listen to the radio and/or television announcements before leaving home regarding the Albuquerque Public Schools (APS) schedule for the day. All CABQ CDCs will follow the announced APS schedule. If APS announces a two-hour delay, all CABQ CDCs will begin accepting children at 10:00am. Breakfast will not be served. If APS announces an early dismissal due to snow or other bad weather, all CABQ CDCs will also close and contact families to pick up their child. If there is severe weather and APS happens to be on break, call the center before leaving home. If APS announces a closure, all centers will close.

entries.title: Attendance and Absences
entries.body: Children are expected to attend the center full time which is a total of 6.5 hours per day 5 days a week. It is the parent's responsibility to notify the center daily if your child is going to be absent. Notify the Head Teacher if your child will be out for a long period due to illness, vacation or family emergency. If you have not called the center after two consecutive weeks of absence and there has been no contact, your child may be disenrolled.

entries.title: Arrival and Departure Procedures
entries.body: Your child must be accompanied by an adult to and from the classroom and/or parking area. Per CYFD NM State Regulations, children must be accompanied to and picked up from their rooms by an adult (age 18 and over). Each child must be signed in and out by a parent or designated adult listed on the pick-up list. Only people who are on the written pick-up list and are 18 years of age (unless a parent) and older will be allowed to pick up a child. People on your list will be asked to show an ID. You must sign your child in and out (including time of day) upon arrival or departure daily. Inform teaching staff upon arrival/departure. Each parent/guardian must remind their child to wash their hands upon arrival and departure to prevent the spreading of germs.

entries.title: Late Arrival, Late Pick-Up, and $15 Late Fee
entries.body: Children must arrive on time. If an emergency arises and you will be late, call the center. After three (3) late arrivals or pick-ups, a parent conference will be scheduled. Continued late arrivals/pick-ups may result in disenrollment. If your child is left at the center after the center or program has closed, every effort will be made to contact family members and emergency contacts. If we are unable to reach anyone and you have still not contacted us 30 minutes after closing, Head Teachers and Teachers will contact the Albuquerque Police Department who will call Child Protective Services. Teachers will also notify the Education Specialist and/or Program Manager and contact State Licensing. Any parent picking up a child while suspected of being under the influence of a substance will also be reported to the Albuquerque Police Department and Child Protective Services. Late pick-up fee: $15.00 per occurrence, added to your online bill.

entries.title: Tuition, Fees, and Payment
entries.body: All families enrolled have an established fee based on a graduated fee schedule determined by family income and number of family members. There is an additional fee for siblings and fees for NM Pre-K Extended Care. Fees are minimal and non-refundable; there is no daily pro-rating of fees. Established/required fees must be paid in advance of services regardless of attendance — families are paying for the spot their child is enrolled in, so fees must be paid regardless of attendance. If using state subsidy, documentation of a state subsidy contract is required at enrollment. Fees are paid in advance online. For assistance or to pay by check or money order, payments can be made at: The Office of Child and Family Development, 1820 Randolph Rd SE Albuquerque, NM 87106, Monday – Friday 8:00 a.m. – 4:30 p.m. only. Cash is accepted at the Office of Child and Family Development. City of Albuquerque Treasury does not allow change for cash payments — bring exact cash. Check or money orders payable to the City of Albuquerque. Late pick-up fee is $15.00 per occurrence.

entries.title: Early Head Start Enrollment (Center Based)
entries.body: Requirements for center based EHS enrollment: 1) Child must be between the ages of six weeks and three years of age. 2) Parents must be in compliance with EHS guidelines i.e. assessments, home visits, individual and family development plans. 3) Parents/guardians must attend a Mandatory Enrollment Meeting to fill out application forms and orientation to the parent handbook before the child begins attending. (For Home Based options contact the EHS Intake Specialist.) 4) Two copies of the Parental Agreement are signed. 5) Required documentation: copies of immunization records, birth certificate, and emergency contact numbers. 6) Vacation time for up to two weeks will not jeopardize center enrollment. Eligibility documentation can include check/pay stubs, 1099 IRS Form/Quarterly Taxes if self-employed, and letters of employment.

entries.title: Preschool and NM Pre-K Enrollment Process
entries.body: Enrollment takes place through a series of steps: 1) Each family completes a phone prequalification with the enrollment office. 2) Family provides documentation and signs paperwork about the qualifying terms. 3) Family receives the Parent Handbook. 4) Enrolling parent signs a contract listing their established fee and acknowledgement of the handbook. 5) Family participates in any required parent meetings. 6) All forms must be completed in full and signed before the child is enrolled. Qualifications: For Preschool the child must be three to five years of age. For NM Early Pre-K, the child must be 3 years old by Sept 1st. For NM Pre-K, the child must be 4 years old by Sept 1st. Required enrollment documents can include: child's birth certificate, child's immunization records, emergency contact information, ID, check/pay stubs, 1099 IRS Form if self-employed, Quarterly Taxes, written statement of Employment.

entries.title: Enrolling Parent Role
entries.body: Each family will choose an enrolling parent. The Enrolling Parent is the primary contact for information about the child and center events, closures, activities, etc. They are responsible for all fees and written documentation. The Enrolling Parent chooses the people on the child's emergency contact and child release (pick-up) list. Upon enrollment, consideration will be paid to court-ordered documentation that refers to the parenting plans in place.

entries.title: Disenrollment and Expulsion
entries.body: Families may experience disenrollment/expulsion. A conference is held with family, Education Specialist, and Program Manager. Reasons include: 1) Non-payment or habitual late payment of fees. 2) Failure to follow EHS, Preschool, or NM Pre-K practices and procedures. 3) Failure to attend required parent participation activities. 4) Child has not attended for two consecutive weeks with no contact, nonpayment, or constant absenteeism. 5) Child's behavior causes harm to self, other children, property, or staff with no successful outcome. 6) Enrolling parent or family representative exhibits abusive/inappropriate behavior toward CABQ program staff or participants. 7) Negative interactions between parents or divorced families that interfere with normal functions of the center.

entries.title: Custody Issues for Divorced/Separated Parents
entries.body: During enrollment, please explain the custody arrangements and bring legal documentation or court orders that include the parenting plan in place. DCFD has an obligation to remain a neutral party. Only people placed on the emergency contacts and child release lists by the enrolling parent will have access to the child unless there is a court ordered parenting plan. If there is a dispute about parental rights, families must take care of that communication off site. Authorities may be called to settle disagreements. As custody issues are legally decided, documentation of the settlement must be left with the enrollment office and center. Restraining orders must be on file. Remember the center is a neutral party — overnight bags, weekend luggage, car seats, etc. should not be stored at the center. If situations occur between parents that interfere with normal functions, it may be grounds for disenrollment.

entries.title: Preschool and NM Pre-K Student Transfers
entries.body: For families requesting a transfer from one CDC to another: 1) Notify your child's Head Teacher. 2) Any outstanding balance must be paid in full. 3) Head Teacher informs Enrollment Specialist and submits a status change form. 4) Enrollment office determines availability of a spot. 5) Enrollment Specialist informs Education Specialist for continuity of services. 6) Families that transfer go through requalification. 7) All information about the child, including IEPs, will follow the child to the next center.

entries.title: Immunization Requirements
entries.body: State regulations require an up-to-date copy of each child's immunization record on file. For exempt status, a current exemption certificate must be on file. Children cannot attend the program without this documentation. CYFD NM State Licensing conducts random checks of child files. New Mexico Immunization Exemption Law (24-5-3): Any minor child through his parent or guardian may file with the health authority charged with the duty of enforcing the immunization laws. 1) A certificate of a duly licensed physician stating that the physical condition of the child is such that immunization would seriously endanger the life or health of the child; or 2) Affidavits or written affirmation from an officer of a recognized religious denomination that such child's parents or guardians are bona fide members of a denomination whose religious teaching requires reliance upon prayers or spiritual means alone for healing; 3) Affidavits or written affirmation from his parent or legal guardian that his religious beliefs, held either individually or jointly with others, do no permit the administration of vaccine or other immunizing agent. NMAC 7.5.3: Within sixty (60) days of receipt of a request for exemption from immunization, the director of the public health division or the designee shall review the certificate to determine whether the certificate has been duly completed.

entries.title: Illness Policy: When to Keep Your Child Home
entries.body: Per State Licensing Regulations and DCFD policy, keep your child at home if he/she has the following: Is feverish or has had a fever within the last 24 hours (100.4); Coughs continuously during naptime; Has vomited or had diarrhea within 24 hours of going to the center; Has pink eye or symptoms which might be pink eye (conjunctivitis); Had developed a runny nose that is cloudy not clear; Has been on antibiotics for less than 24 hours; Has symptoms of any contagious diseases or infections (ringworm, crusted or oozing lesions, chicken pox, etc.); Has an undiagnosed rash. You will be called to pick up your child if any of these symptoms appear. A doctor's note may be required to return to the center. Children who are hospitalized or in emergency room care must have a medical release document from the doctor in order to return to the center.

entries.title: Contagious Diseases Reporting
entries.body: Per CYFD NM State Licensing regulations, the center should be informed when a child has a contagious disease like strep, chicken pox, pink eye, etc. Other parents may need to be informed. Families whose child has documentation releasing them from immunizations or whose child has yet to receive an immunization due to age must stay home to protect the child until the contagion has been contained. Children who are under-immunized and seem to show symptoms of a vaccine-preventable disease will be sent home. Certain contagious diseases must be reported by the center to State Licensing and to the Department of Public Health, who will contact the family. A list of reportable diseases is posted in each center.

entries.title: Medication Administration Policy
entries.body: The center may administer medication with written permission from the parent. The permission form requires the name of the child, the medication, the dosage, and the hours the medication is to be given. Any medication brought to the Center must be in the original container, labeled with the child's name and given to a teacher to securely store. This includes: sun screen, lotion, lip balm, diaper rash ointment, teething treatments, children's Tylenol. No cough drops will be administered due to the choking hazard. Medication will only be given by a teacher (or teacher designee) and a written record is kept for six months. Parents must initial the medication form daily. Medication cannot be placed in the child's cubby, backpacks, etc. Medication is stored in a secure container away from the reach of children. The program reserves the right to refuse to give medication. Due to a child's specific need or disability, parents are responsible for providing staff training by medical personnel for medications that require training.

entries.title: Sunscreen Policy
entries.body: All children enrolled in DCFD programs receive sun protection during sun exposure of more than 10 minutes at one time. Steps: Protect children from the sun, seek shaded areas; Cover up with clothing; Apply a hat that shades the face, scalp, ears and neck; Obtain a signed parent permission to apply sunscreen; Apply sunscreen with SPF 15-30 UVA and UVB protection 30 minutes before child goes outside. Parents bring in and label sunscreen for the individual child with the child's name; sunscreen will be stored out of reach. Observe the skin for signs of irritation; discontinue use and notify parent.

entries.title: Nutrition, Meals, and CACFP
entries.body: DCFD programs follow the recommended meal pattern standards set by USDA. All children's meals are funded by the Child and Adult Care Food Program (CACFP). Food is provided by Canteen. Early Head Start provides formula with iron. If your child is breastfed, you will need to provide a supply of expressed breast milk; breast milk must be labeled and dated. Infants on breast milk must show the ability to successfully bottle feed. Children and parents please do not bring outside food into the center. Exceptions to bringing outside food will be decided by the Head Teacher (outside food must be store-bought with a nutrition label). The CDCs limit the use of peanut products and request peanut-free snacks and 100% juice for children. Meals served family style.

entries.title: Food Allergies
entries.body: If a child has a food allergy, a Nutrition/Allergy Form must be signed by the doctor and given to the Head Teacher for assessment. This information will be shared with the meal vendor so that they can find the right food components to meet the child's needs. Vendors make and serve special meals based on the medical needs of a child; however, they do not make and provide meals based on lifestyle choices. The program assists in informing the vendor about special diets, foods, or other dietary requirements with appropriate documentation from the child's physician. The program also cooperates with diet restrictions for religious or cultural purposes when able.

entries.title: Birthday Celebrations
entries.body: If your child wishes to share his/her birthday with classmates, you are welcome to bring a special treat. The center encourages creative, peanut-free, nutritious ideas. Make arrangements with your child's teacher well in advance to assure all issues are addressed (e.g. religious beliefs, allergies) and all food items brought are store-bought with a nutrition label. Label items with your child's name and the day's date. If you are having a party at home and only some of the children in the classroom are invited, please do not hand out invitations at the center.

entries.title: Early Head Start Daily Schedule (Sample)
entries.body: Ages 0-3 Child Development Center Hours of Operation 8:00 am through 4:30 pm. Daily schedule: 8:00-9:00 a.m. Welcoming the day; breakfast served. 9:00-9:15 Clean up and brush teeth. 9:15-10:30 Program Time: group instruction and small-group activities. 10:30-11:15 Outdoor Play. 11:15-11:30 Bathroom and Hand Washing. 11:30-12:00 p.m. Lunch served family style. 12:00-12:45 Indoor or outdoor explore time. 12:45-1:30 Rest time. 1:30-2:30 Hand Washing and Snack time, small group time, story time, music. Departure time for Early Head Start. 2:30-5:30 p.m. CABQ Extended Care Services. Early Head Start meets the adult/child ratio of 1:4 for toddlers and infants.

entries.title: Preschool and NM Pre-K Daily Schedule (Sample) and Ratios
entries.body: Ages 3-5 with Center Hours of Operation 7:00 am through 5:30 pm. 7:00-8:00 a.m. Welcoming the day; AM NM Pre-K Extended Care. 8:00-9:00 Breakfast. 9:00-9:15 Clean up and brush teeth. 9:15-10:30 Program Time. 10:30-11:15 Outdoor Play. 11:15-11:30 Bathroom and Hand Washing. 11:30-12:00 p.m. Lunch family style. 12:00-12:45 Indoor/outdoor explore. 12:45-1:30 Rest time. 1:30-2:30 Small group time, story, music. Departure time for NM Pre-K and pm CABQ Extended Care. 2:30-3:00 Hand Washing and Snack. 3:00-5:30 Afternoon experiences. For Extended Care, please arrive no later than 9:00 am. Preschool meets the adult/child ratio of 1/12 and NM Pre-K meets the adult/child ratio of 1/10.

entries.title: Curriculum: Creative Curriculum and Assessment
entries.body: Each EHS Center plans curriculum around children's interests using Creative Curriculum and the Ages and Stages Questionnaire (ASQ) for ages birth to three. Teaching Strategies is the on-going assessment database for Creative Curriculum. New Mexico Early Learning Guidelines help teachers identify development across seven areas. Preschool and NM Pre-K use Creative Curriculum for ages three through five. For NM Pre-K, centers implement specific state-mandated curriculum standards. Assessment tools include the Ages and Stages Questionnaire, NM Early Learning Guidelines, Focused Portfolios (observation-based), and the Nurtured Heart Approach for behavioral guidance. Referrals are made to Child Find if needed.

entries.title: Transition to Kindergarten
entries.body: Children ready to transition to kindergarten will take a field trip to visit a kindergarten classroom. Parent meetings will be offered on the topics of transition to kindergarten, school expectations and school readiness. In the springtime, parents will be asked which elementary school their child will be attending, and parents will be notified about important pre-registration dates for kindergarten enrollment in the public school system.

entries.title: Naps and Rest Time
entries.body: Naps and rest time are required by child care licensing regulations. Teaching staff creates a quiet, darkened room with sleep mats for each child. Children who do not fall asleep may rest quietly. Parents may bring a small blanket and small pillow for their child which will be sent home to be laundered weekly. Infant schedules will differ and are based on individual needs; infants nap and eat on demand.

entries.title: Field Trips and Walking Trips
entries.body: The CDCs will periodically schedule field trips. Field trips will involve transportation on a minibus when available; individual parent permission forms will be required. When a minibus is not available, parents may be asked to drive their own child to the field trip. Teachers will notify parents each time a field trip is scheduled. Field trips are not mandatory — if you do not want your child to attend, let staff know in advance so arrangements can be made to take your child to a nearby CABQ CDC. Walking Trips: CDC staff plan walking field trips weather permitting. Parental permission is required (the walking field trip permission form is part of the enrollment packet). These trips are restricted to a 4-block radius of the center and do not involve crossing streets with heavy traffic.

entries.title: Child Seat and Seat Belt Safety
entries.body: By law: Children are not allowed to be unattended in the parking lot or in cars. Children under one year of age shall be properly secured in a rear-facing child passenger restraining device that meets federal standards in the rear seat of the vehicle. If there is no rear seat, the child may ride in the front seat if the passenger-side air bag is deactivated. Children 1 year to age 4, regardless of weight, or children who weigh less than 40 pounds shall be secured in a child passenger restraint device. Children 5 or 6 must ride in a child safety seat or booster seat. Children 40-60 lbs., regardless of age, shall be properly secured in a child safety seat or child booster seat. Children 7 through 12 years of age shall be properly secured in a child safety seat, booster seat or a seat belt. A child age 13 through 17 must ride in a seat belt. Due to limited space, car seats may NOT be left or stored at the center.

entries.title: Emergency Procedures and Safety Preparedness
entries.body: Child Development Centers follow emergency procedures in their Safety Preparedness Plans. In the event of a major emergency (fire, tornado, bomb threats, severe weather, etc.) teaching staff will follow emergency procedures and evacuate to a safe off-site location if needed. Parents will be called to pick up children. When the center cannot operate in compliance with State Regulations (water/gas/power shut off) parents will be called. For continuity of operations, a placement at another center may be arranged if possible. Steps posted in each classroom: 1) Administer First Aid as needed. 2) Call 911 if needed. 3) Call the parent or emergency contacts. 4) Call DCFD Main Office. 5) Notify State Licensing. 6) Evacuate to a safe location if necessary. The teaching staff at the centers all have First Aid and CPR certification.

entries.title: Center Lock-Down / Shelter in Place
entries.body: A Center can be in a lock-down status due to a dangerous situation. For safety reasons no person may enter or leave the classroom until the lock-down is lifted or until the police make instructions clear. The teaching staff will make every effort to keep children calm and safe. Children will be occupied with play activities. Teaching staff might not be allowed to use the telephone depending on the situation. You may contact the DCFD main office for more information about the lock down at 767-6500. For your safety and the safety of the children, please stay in your vehicle until the lock down has been lifted.

entries.title: Suspected Child Abuse and Neglect Reporting
entries.body: In observance of New Mexico laws, Teaching Staff are trained to observe children who are under their care for any changes in behavior or physical evidence which might indicate child abuse or neglect. They are required by law to report to the Children, Youth & Families Department. Teaching Staff will also notify the DCFD main office supervisors and State Licensing and provide required documentation and incident reports. Concerns about child abuse or neglect are not confidential because such concerns are required by state law to be immediately reported to the proper authorities.

entries.title: Supervision and Missing Child Procedure
entries.body: Children must be adequately supervised by sight indoors and outdoors at all times. Attendance is checked regularly throughout the day. If a child cannot be found after a thorough search of the facility indoors and out: 1) 911 will be called. 2) Parents will be notified immediately. 3) A call to the DCFD main office will be placed. 4) A call to the State of New Mexico Child Care Licensing Bureau will be placed. 5) For centers on APS grounds, the principal will also be notified.

entries.title: Children with Special Needs: IFSP and IEP
entries.body: The DCFD program provides services for children with special needs and follows a policy of inclusion in regular classroom activities to the greatest extent possible. For children ages three and under, an Individual Family Service Plan (IFSP) is developed with the participation of service providers, staff, health professionals, and the parents. The IFSP guides and monitors development throughout the year. For Preschool children, an Individual Education Plan (IEP) is written to develop individualized care and education. A parent or family member may be asked to attend school with the child for a limited time to help with special needs and ease the child's adjustment to the center.

entries.title: Toilet Learning
entries.body: DCFD believes in a partnership with parents in toilet learning. In Early Head Start, the time to begin toilet teaching is decided largely by parents based on signs of readiness: physical readiness (stay dry overnight), regular bowel movements, increased awareness of body functions, understands wet/dry, clean/dirty, up/down, can communicate needs, can dress self with minimal assistance, curious about bathroom habits of others. Schedule a 10-15 minute conference with the Head Teacher. In Preschool and NM Pre-K, families meet with the Head Teacher and Education Specialist to create a Toilet Learning plan. Parents must provide pull ups and extra clothing. Teachers will dispose of soiled pull-ups and put soiled clothing in a zip lock bag for family collection. A daily progress report is provided.

entries.title: Diapers, Extra Clothes, and Manageable Clothing
entries.body: Early Head Start provides diapers while the child is in attendance in any Early Head Start center, in accordance with the Head Start Performance Standards. Extra Clothes: An extra set of clothing items must be labeled with your child's name and left at the Child Development Center at all times. Provide shirt, pants, socks, and underpants. Manageable Clothing: avoid bodysuits, heavy belts, overalls, and clothing that fastens in the back, on the shoulder, or in between the legs unless the child is able to manage it. Open-toe sandals may present a danger and should be avoided. Closed-toe sandals or tennis shoes are preferred. Shoes with roller wheels are NOT allowed at the center.

entries.title: Guidance and Discipline Policy
entries.body: Guidance/discipline procedures are consistent with the State of New Mexico Child Care Regulations: "Discipline means guidance which fosters the child's ability to become self-disciplined, demonstrating orderly conduct in relationship to peers and adults. Discipline shall be clear and understandable to the child, consistent and explained to the child before and at the time of any disciplinary action. Discipline includes positive guidance, redirection and the setting of clear-cut limits, which foster the child's own ability to become self-disciplined." Preschool teaching staff uses the Nurtured Heart Approach, focusing on positive behaviors. Practices which are prohibited include: Physical punishment of any kind including shaking, biting, hitting, pinching or putting anything on or in child's mouth; Withdrawal of food, rest or bathroom opportunities; Abusive or profane language, any form of public or private humiliation including threats of physical punishment; Unsupervised isolation of the child; Any other type of punishment that is hazardous to the physical or mental health of the child.

entries.title: Behavioral Issues and Restraint
entries.body: Children whose behavior poses a threat to themselves and/or others may be physically restrained by staff members and held quietly until they regain self-control. If a pattern of aggressive behavior emerges and the child's behavior harms himself, other children, teachers or property, the teacher in consultation with the family, center and/or program management and program health, mental health and/or disabilities resources will schedule a conference with the parents to decide how to address the situation. Every effort is made to ensure a child's continued enrollment. When all reasonable attempts have been made and there is no positive outcome, a decision may be made that the program is not the most appropriate placement. For Early Head Start, a recommendation to continue services through home based can be made.

entries.title: Parent Participation Requirements
entries.body: Required of Preschool/Pre-K parents: One Orientation to the classroom and center; A home visit; Attendance at three (3) parent/teacher conferences per year; Two parenting workshops; Two of additional opportunities (field trips, family social gatherings, volunteering). Early Head Start Parent Engagement includes: Two Home Visits (six months apart, required by Head Start Performance Standards), Two Parent Teacher Conferences annually, opportunities to volunteer, participate in Policy Council, regularly-scheduled parent meetings, family events. Preschool and NM Pre-K classrooms close three (3) times during the year to accommodate conferences.

entries.title: Parent Visitors and Open-Door Policy
entries.body: Parents are welcome to visit their child's classroom at any time. If you visit during the day, plan to stay at least 30 minutes to give your child plenty of time to share their classroom experience. Other family members may also visit. All visitors will be asked to sign in. Please schedule a meeting with your child's teacher if you need to communicate information that may take longer than a ten minute time frame.

entries.title: Inquiries, Concerns, and Grievance Process
entries.body: The Head Teacher at each CABQ CDC is also a licensed Director by the State of New Mexico and is the person in charge of day-to-day operations. Parents are urged to communicate directly with the Head Teacher when they have inquiries, ideas or concerns. Any complaints should first be directed to the Teacher in your child's classroom. The Head Teacher will hear and mediate. If this is not sufficient, please submit your concerns in writing to the Education Specialist and/or the Program Manager at the DCFD main office, 1820 Randolph SE, Albuquerque, NM 87106. You are also welcome to call the DCFD and speak to the Child Development Program Education Specialist and/or Program Manager at 767-6500.

entries.title: Phone Calls and Cell Phone Use
entries.body: During program time, center staff are working with and teaching children. If you need to communicate with teachers or with your child, call the center and leave a message. If there is an emergency, please state the emergency in a message on the machine and center staff will respond at once. Cell phone usage by parents or teaching staff is not allowed in the child development center. Quality greetings, interactions and program time with all adults and other children are important while at the center. Emergency phone calls are the exception and should be taken in private and away from the group to ensure confidentiality.

entries.title: Media Release Statement
entries.body: DCFD centers may use children's photos and/or family photos in their programs. These images both in print and electronic, still or live may be used for the purpose of educational training, media coverage, classroom display or individual child portfolios. When any such videotape, film or photograph (digital or film) is produced and released, it becomes the property of the party to whom it is released and may be replayed or reprinted at a later date. Images posted by the City of Albuquerque on the City's internet website will be used without names for security measures. Photos/video may also be unintentionally taken and used on social media platforms. The City of Albuquerque is not responsible for how photos/videos are inadvertently used.

entries.title: Items Brought From Home and Pets in the Classroom
entries.body: Please do not bring toys or personal items as they may become broken, misplaced or damaged. A book for sharing with the class or a stuffed animal for naptime can be brought and must be marked with the child's name. Items not allowed: jewelry, tattoo stickers, fake fingernails, make-up, lip gloss, lip balm as make-up, toys. The centers discourage violent play — children are not permitted to bring toy guns or war toys. Pets in the Classroom: Some Preschool and NM Pre-K programs may have pets in the classroom; as a precaution Early Head Start does not allow pets in the classroom.

entries.title: Smoking Policy and Pest Control
entries.body: Smoking is not allowed at City of Albuquerque facilities; this includes e-cigarette use. The DCFD participates in routine pest control services. The schedule for these services is posted at each center.

entries.title: Governing Regulations and Nondiscrimination
entries.body: City of Albuquerque Child Development centers operate under the provisions of "New Mexico State Regulations Governing Facilities Providing Day/Night Care to Children." Early Head Start centers also operate under "The Head Start Act" and "Head Start Programs Performance Standards." State regulations are available upon request from the Children, Youth and Families Department, State of New Mexico. Centers also operate under City of Albuquerque local government protocol and Administrative Instruction. Per the Zero Tolerance Policy (Administrative Instruction No. 7-22): "It is the policy of the City of Albuquerque that workplace violence by its employees shall be prohibited. Furthermore, violent behavior directed toward a City employee by a member of the general public shall not be tolerated." In accordance with Federal law and U.S. Department of Agriculture policy, the City does not discriminate on the basis of race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability.
